message:
fcp-store: prevent starvation in repair queue via deferral aging

Audit of the fcp-store repair planner / queue surfaced one concrete
scheduling defect.

[MEDIUM] RepairController queue starves low-priority items under churn

  `RepairController` sorted its queue purely by `priority` (descending),
  with `object_id` as tie-break. Under heavy churn — a zone where
  evaluate_zone keeps surfacing fresh high-priority repairs faster
  than `next_repair` can drain at the configured rate — a
  low-priority item sits at the back of the queue indefinitely.
  Because `upsert_repair` OVERWROTE the existing entry on Refresh, an
  item that had been waiting through many evaluate cycles had no
  bookkeeping that would lift it above fresh arrivals. That's
  classic priority starvation.

  Fix: wrap RepairRequest in a private `QueuedRepair { request,
  deferral_count }`. When `upsert_repair` finds an existing entry for
  the same object, it bumps `deferral_count` (saturating) instead of
  silently resetting the history. `sort_queue` orders by
  `effective_priority = priority + deferral_count * AGING_BOOST_PER_DEFERRAL`
  (saturating_mul / saturating_add), so a P=500 item that has been
  refreshed through 5+ evaluate cycles beats fresh P=1000 arrivals.

  AGING_BOOST_PER_DEFERRAL=100 and MAX_DEFERRAL_COUNT=1_000_000 keep
  the arithmetic bounded. Aging is reset to 0 on removal
  (next_repair, remove_queued_repair, prune_zone_repairs,
  clear_queue) — only items that survive re-upsert carry an aging
  penalty.

  Public API surface is unchanged: `RepairRequest` retains its exact
  fields, `queue_repair`/`next_repair`/`queue_depth` have identical
  signatures. The QueuedRepair wrapper is crate-private.

Regression tests:
- aging_lifts_persistently_deferred_low_priority_above_fresh_high_priority
  The motivating scenario: a P=500 item upserted through 10 evaluate
  cycles (deferral_count=10, effective=1500) MUST drain before a
  fresh P=1000 arrival (deferral_count=0, effective=1000). Prior
  behavior would always pick P=1000 first.
- aging_does_not_invert_priority_on_fresh_inputs
  Guardrail: fresh P=1000 MUST still win over fresh P=500. Aging is
  a cross-cycle tie-breaker, not a global inversion.

Not patched (noted for follow-up):
- [LOW] GC `collect_internal` materializes `visited`, `live`, and
  `all_objects` in memory; O(N) per zone. Fine for current scale,
  but streaming iteration would help at 10M+ objects per zone.
- [LOW] GC eviction budget cap applies in ObjectId sort order with
  no cross-cycle tracking of which IDs were deferred last run.
  Fresh objects with earlier-sorting IDs under heavy churn can
  delay later-sorted objects — same class of fairness issue as
  the repair queue, but less acute because ObjectIds don't typically
  cluster by arrival time.

Clean findings (no patch):
- `collect_internal` BFS has no length-prefix / unbounded-read
  vulnerability; the queue only holds already-visited ObjectIds.
- Planner's `plan_zone` priority sort uses stable tie-breakers
  (estimated_bytes → decode_ms → object_id), deterministic.
- `can_fit` uses saturating arithmetic for budget checks (no
  wraparound possible for pathological `estimated_bytes`).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
